articles.description: From AI-generated images to restricted satellite data, the systems used to verify what’s real online are struggling to keep up.

articles.title: Two simple eating habits linked to lower weight, study finds
articles.description: A major study suggests that when you eat could play a key role in staying lean. People who fast longer overnight and start their day with an early breakfast were more likely to have a lower BMI years later. Scientists think this is because eating earlier aligns better with the body’s internal clock.

articles.title: Unusual airborne toxin detected in the U.S. for the first time
articles.description: Scientists searching for air pollution clues stumbled onto something unexpected: toxic MCCPs drifting through the air for the first time in the Western Hemisphere. The likely source—fertilizer made from sewage sludge—points to a hidden route for contamination.

articles.title: A 67-year-old “crazy” theory about vitamin B1 has finally been proven
articles.description: Scientists have achieved the unthinkable by stabilizing a highly reactive molecule in water, confirming a decades-old theory about vitamin B1’s role in the body. The breakthrough not only solves a scientific mystery but could revolutionize greener chemical manufacturing.

articles.title: Your nose could detect Alzheimer’s years before symptoms begin
articles.description: Losing your sense of smell might signal Alzheimer’s far earlier than expected. Scientists found that immune cells in the brain actively destroy smell-related nerve fibers after detecting abnormal signals on their surfaces. articles.description: Lake Powell, the second-largest human-made reservoir in the US, has lost nearly 7% of its potential storage capacity since 1963, when Glen Canyon Dam was built, a new report shows.
